title: podman container root able to write to file with no write permissions
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->podman version 2.2.1

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
I am able to write to a file, when in bash process within container, when that file has no write permissions.  The file is 0400.
The container is rootless.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. pull python latest image from dockerhub.  Run container with podman run -d -it --pod $POD_NAME --name $CONTAINER_NAME -v $WORKING_DIR:/opt/$DJANGO_PROJECT_NAME:Z  $IMAGE

2. run  podman exec -it $CONTAINER_NAME bash

3. create file, set permissions to 0400, run nano (installed with apt-get) on the file, write some stuff, save and exit.

**Describe the results you received:**
a file with some stuff in it.

**Describe the results you expected:**
an empty file, with nano faulting, stating 'no write permissions'

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 2.2.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**


**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
using rpm-ostree, running on fedora-IOT-aarch64.  Have pinned podman version to 2.2.1 as the later 3.0.1 (current), does not allow me to connect successfully to a container in a pod -- issue #9532 
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

No, cannot.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Are you running as root, or without?

I believe this would happen without containers at all.  Try this outside of a container and the same thing should happen.  The issue is you have the ability to write to the directory, and NANO is probably creating a temporary file and then renaming over you file, which does not take the permissions of the existing file into account.

Sorry, I should have mentioned, I am running rootless.

On Fri, 12 Mar 2021, 19:13 Matthew Heon, ***@***.***> wrote:

> Are you running as root, or without?
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you authored the thread.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/9704#issuecomment-797695061>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AA4GSGI4OHVGZMFVSDGKYO3TDJKWDANCNFSM4ZCZGGQA>
> .
>

wowzers.  I just checked on my linux, and root can indeed write to files without permissions set.  I knew root would have the power to change the permissions and then write, but I didn't know that root could ignore permissions and just write regardless... doh.
many thanks for your help.
